observations: ct-input renders a single native <input> and no images, icons, or other non-text content of its own.

standard: 1.2.3 Audio Description or Media Alternative (Prerecorded) (Level A)

observations: ct-input renders only the bare <input> — it has no visible-label mechanism of its own (that is ct-label's role). Because ct-input renders into its own shadow root, a sibling <ct-label for="x">/<label for="x"> in the light DOM cannot form a working programmatic association with it: id references are resolved within a single tree scope and do not cross shadow-DOM boundaries, and a plain custom-element host (no ElementInternals form association) is not part of the browser's 'labelable element' set either way. The only association mechanism this component supports on its own is the `aria-label` property, which is forwarded directly onto the rendered <input>. The same cross-shadow-root limitation applies to pairing with ct-field-message via `aria-describedby` (see 3.3.1/3.3.3) — no id set on either component's host is reachable from inside the other's shadow root. A future civictheme:field molecule composing ct-label + ct-input + ct-field-message in one place, or a cross-root ARIA mechanism (e.g. ElementInternals' ariaLabelledByElements once broadly supported), is needed to close this gap; until then, `aria-label` is the only reliable option.

The `autocomplete` attribute this criterion concerns is not currently exposed as a property on ct-input (it mirrors CivicTheme's own input.component.yml, which likewise has no autocomplete field) and there is no attribute-passthrough mechanism to set it on the rendered <input> inside the shadow root. This is not just an implementer responsibility left open — it is currently impossible to satisfy through this component's public API for common input purposes (name, email, tel, etc.) without a future `autocomplete` property being added.

The `invalid` state is conveyed only through a border/background color change (plus `aria-invalid="true"` for assistive technology) — ct-input renders no icon or text of its own to indicate the error. Compliance depends on the implementer always pairing an invalid ct-input with a visible text description of the problem (e.g. an adjacent ct-field-message), not relying on the red border alone for sighted users.

Both the default text color (--ct-input-light/dark-color) and the placeholder color (explicitly set to the same value, not the browser's lighter default gray) meet the minimum 4.5:1 contrast ratio against the input's background in both themes, using the same CivicTheme body-text/background token pairing already relied on elsewhere in this design system.
Text is still visible and readable when resized to at least 200%, since sizing is driven by rem-based typography tokens.
All text (value, placeholder) is rendered as real text by the native <input>, never as an image.
The input is fluid (width: 100%) with no fixed pixel width, except the native type="color" swatch which keeps a small fixed footprint by design (matching the native browser control).

The border color in both the default and focus-visible states, and the invalid-state border color, meet 3:1 contrast against the input's own background in both light and dark themes, using CivicTheme's existing border/interaction/error tokens.

Text remains visible/readable when line spacing, letter spacing, and word spacing are increased per this criterion's thresholds, since no fixed height or overflow:hidden clips the value/placeholder text.

standard: 1.4.13 Content on Hover or Focus (Level AA)
observations: ct-input does not introduce any additional content on hover or focus beyond the native focus-visible outline.
The rendered element is a native <input>, fully operable via keyboard (focus, type, select) with no custom event handling that could interfere.
The native <input> introduces no keyboard trap; focus moves in and out normally via Tab/Shift+Tab.

standard: 2.3.1 Three Flashes or Below Threshold (Level A)

observations: ct-input is a single focusable element that participates in the natural document tab order; it does not manipulate tabindex or focus order itself.
ct-input renders no links.

ct-input renders no visible label/heading text of its own (that is ct-label's role — see 1.3.1). The only text this component contributes that could be evaluated against this criterion is whatever string an implementer supplies via `aria-label`; compliance depends on that string clearly and specifically describing the field's purpose (e.g. "Email address", not "Field" or "Input").
The component's own CSS always renders a solid focus-visible outline (`--ct-input-{theme}-outline-color`, width/offset tokens) regardless of implementer configuration.
Nothing in ct-input's own rendering overlaps or hides its focus indicator; obscuring it would require page-level layout (e.g. a sticky header) outside this component's control.
Activation uses the native <input> focus/click behavior only; no custom pointerdown-triggered action is attached.

If an implementer supplies both a visible label (via an adjacent ct-label, despite the association caveat in 1.3.1) and an `aria-label` on ct-input, the `aria-label` text must still contain the visible label's text verbatim. Simplest compliant approach: rely on `aria-label` alone as the sole accessible name rather than layering a second, potentially divergent, visible label string.

With the default typography/padding tokens (0.8125rem vertical padding + 1.25rem line-height + border), the rendered control is roughly 48px tall and full-width, well over the 24x24 CSS pixel minimum. The narrower type="color" swatch (~46x35px) also clears the minimum in both dimensions.
Compliance depends on the implementer setting the correct lang attribute on the html element; not something a single form control can affect.
Compliance depends on the implementer setting lang attributes on parts of the page that differ from the main language (e.g. a placeholder or value in a different language than the surrounding page).
ct-input introduces no side effects on focus beyond the native focus-visible style change — no auto-navigation, auto-submission, or other unexpected context change.
ct-input introduces no side effects on value change beyond dispatching the native `input`/`change` events — no auto-submission or forced navigation is bundled into the component.
Compliance depends on the implementer using the same `type`, `aria-label`, and visual presentation for the same kind of field everywhere it recurs across the site.

Setting `invalid` gives the field a red border and `aria-invalid="true"`, which identifies *that* an error exists, but ct-input renders no text describing *what* the error is. Compliance depends on the implementer pairing the invalid input with a text description (e.g. ct-field-message) — and, per the 1.3.1 caveat, `aria-describedby` pointing from ct-input's inner <input> to an id on a sibling custom element's host will not resolve across the shadow-DOM boundary either, so today there is no working *programmatic* link between the two; only their visual adjacency conveys the relationship.

ct-input provides no instructional text of its own; compliance depends on the implementer supplying a label (via `aria-label`, with the caveats noted in 1.3.1/2.4.6) and any necessary format instructions (e.g. via ct-field-message) alongside it.
Compliance depends entirely on the implementer providing a correction suggestion in an accompanying text component (e.g. ct-field-message); ct-input has no mechanism to render suggestion text itself.

Role comes for free from the native <input> element, and state (value, `disabled`, `required`, `aria-invalid`) is always correctly exposed by the component regardless of implementer input. The accessible *name*, however, is only exposed when the implementer sets `aria-label` — per the 1.3.1 caveat, an external <label for> cannot supply it because id references don't cross this component's shadow-DOM boundary. Without `aria-label`, the control has no accessible name at all.
ct-input renders no live-region status messages itself; dynamic validation feedback is ct-field-message's and the implementer's responsibility to expose via aria-live.
